query: requirements for deferred defects under MEL

response.explanation:
Requirements for Deferred Defects under the Minimum Equipment List (MEL)

Requirement What it means Where it comes from
Defect must be identified, recorded and deferred in an approved way When a fault is found, it is logged and the decision to defer it is made according to the MEL’s procedures. Chunk 1 – “Check that the deferred defects have been identified, recorded, and rectified/deferred in accordance with approved procedures and within approved time.”
Deferred defects must be transferred to the next maintenance check The defect is written onto the maintenance worksheet that will be used at the next scheduled check. Chunk 3 – “Deferred defects should be transferred onto worksheets at the next appropriate maintenance check.”
If not fixed at that check, re‑enter the defect The same defect is logged again on a new deferred‑defect record sheet, keeping the original date. Chunk 3 – “any deferred defect which is not rectified during the maintenance check, should be re‑entered on to a new deferred defect record sheet. The original date of the defect should be retained.”
Components needed for repair must be ordered and fitted as soon as possible Parts or components required to fix the defect are prioritized and supplied at the earliest opportunity. Chunk 3 – “The necessary components or parts needed for the rectification of defects should be made available or ordered on a priority basis, and fitted at the earliest opportunity.”
Pilot/flight crew must be informed Whenever possible, the crew is notified of the deferred defect before they take off. Chunk 3 – “All deferred defects should be made known to the pilot/flight crew, whenever possible, prior to their arrival at the aircraft.”
Defects must be listed on the current deferred‑maintenance list The defect appears on the official list (e.g., ML.A.305(d)(6)) and is scheduled for rectification at the next maintenance event. Chunk 5 – “Deferred defects should be listed on the current list of deferred maintenance (ML.A.305(d)(6)) and rectified at the next appropriate maintenance event.”
Defect must be rectified within the limit specified in the maintenance data The MEL or maintenance data gives a time limit; the defect must be fixed before that limit expires. Chunk 5 – “rectified at the next appropriate maintenance event and within the limit specified in the maintenance data.”
MEL application is the responsibility of the operating crew The crew decides whether to accept or reject an operation that has a deferred defect, following the MEL’s application procedure. Chunk 4 – “The MEL is a document not controlled by the CAMO and the decision of whether accepting or not the operation with a defect deferred in accordance with the MEL is normally the responsibility of the operating crew.”

Summary

Under the MEL, a deferred defect must be identified, recorded, and scheduled for repair in a documented way. It is transferred to the next maintenance worksheet, and if not fixed, it is re‑logged with its original date. Parts are ordered on priority, the crew is informed, and the defect appears on the official deferred‑maintenance list. The repair must occur before the time limit set in the maintenance data. The operating crew applies the MEL and decides whether the aircraft may operate with the deferred defect.
